metadata.summary: Six structured patterns for building agentic AI systems: ReAct, Chain-of-Thought, Tool Use, Plan-Execute, Reflection, and Tree-of-Thoughts — plus multi-agent coordination topologies.

content.title: AI Agent Design Patterns
content.summary: Six structured patterns for building agentic AI systems: ReAct (Thought-Action-Observation loop), Chain-of-Thought, Tool Use, Plan-Execute, Reflection, and Tree-of-Thoughts — plus multi-agent coordination topologies (Sequential, Parallel, Supervisor, Hierarchical). Choosing the right pattern at design time is more important than implementation details.

why.p: Ad-hoc "call LLM in a loop" code lacks explicit exit conditions, error recovery, and observability hooks. Named patterns enforce structure: ReAct requires an iteration cap; Reflection requires explicit criteria; Plan-Execute separates planning model from execution model for cost tiering. Structured patterns produce debuggable, cost-predictable agents.

list.item: Selecting an architecture before building any agentic system
Replacing informal LLM loops with debuggable, observable patterns
Multi-step workflows where intermediate results determine next steps (ReAct, Plan-Execute)
Quality-critical outputs where first-pass is insufficient (Reflection)
Multi-agent coordination with specialized workers
Single LLM call with deterministic output — patterns add latency and cost without benefit
Full input fits in one context window with no tool calls — just prompt directly
Real-time inference under 200ms — ReAct, Reflection, and ToT require multiple round-trips
Reproducible outputs for strict auditing — agent non-determinism conflicts with reproducibility requirements

section.name: core-patterns
section.p: ReAct, Chain-of-Thought, Tool Use, and Plan-Execute: when each applies, trade-offs, and critical gotchas.

section.name: react
section.title: ReAct (Reasoning + Acting)

rule.statement: Always set an explicit MAX_TURNS cap on every ReAct loop; never rely on the agent self-terminating.
rule.rationale: Without a cap, an agent on an ambiguous task loops indefinitely. Most frameworks default to unlimited iterations.

section.p: Loop structure: Thought → Action → Observation → repeat until finish action or MAX_TURNS.
Use when: dynamic tasks requiring adaptation, complex multi-step retrieval, actions depend on intermediate results. Trade-off: higher latency (multiple model calls), increased token consumption.

antipattern.description: ReAct loop without error recovery for failed tool observations.
antipattern.reason: A wrong observation at step 3 corrupts all subsequent reasoning. The agent compounds errors rather than recovering.

section.reference: ReAct system prompt with Thought/Action/Observation format

section.name: chain-of-thought
section.title: Chain-of-Thought (CoT)

rule.statement: Use self-consistency voting only when the LLM does NOT have a systematic bias on the task — voting amplifies systematic errors rather than correcting them.
rule.rationale: Self-consistency majority-votes across 3-10 independent samples. If all samples share the same bias, the majority answer is consistently wrong.

section.p: Variants: zero-shot CoT ("Let's think step by step"), few-shot CoT (examples with reasoning traces), self-consistency (multiple paths + majority vote).
Use when: mathematical reasoning, logical deduction, multi-step problem solving, explanation generation.

section.name: tool-use
section.title: Tool Use

rule.statement: Write tool descriptions as the model's only source of truth for when to use the tool; ambiguous or overlapping descriptions cause silent wrong-tool selection.
rule.rationale: The LLM reads tool descriptions verbatim. Vague descriptions like "gets information" do not tell the model which tool to prefer when multiple apply.

rule.statement: Test tool routing explicitly: given a set of representative inputs, verify which tool the model selects — do not rely on post-hoc output inspection.
rule.rationale: Wrong tool selection fails silently; the agent returns a plausible-looking answer from the wrong tool without error.

section.reference: Tool definition schema with description, parameters, required fields

rule.statement: Use a larger model (Opus) for planning and smaller models (Haiku) for mechanical execution steps; synthesize with Sonnet.
rule.rationale: Planning requires deep task decomposition; execution is often mechanical. Model tiering reduces cost by 5-10x vs using Opus throughout.

rule.statement: Validate the task graph for circular dependencies before executing any steps.
rule.rationale: Plan-Execute planners sometimes generate plans with circular dependencies. Executing a circular plan causes infinite loops or deadlocks.

rule.statement: Include a replanning step: if an executor task fails 3 times, return to the planner to revise the task list.
rule.rationale: The planner's task decomposition assumes tool outputs are deterministic. Unexpected failures cascade through the entire plan without replanning.

section.name: advanced-patterns
section.p: Reflection loop calibration, Tree-of-Thoughts pruning, multi-agent topologies, and model tiering across patterns.

section.name: reflection
section.title: Reflection

rule.statement: Provide a concrete pass threshold in the critic prompt (e.g., "≤1 minor defect = pass"); a critic prompted too harshly always finds defects and the loop never terminates.
rule.rationale: Without a concrete exit condition, Reflection loops run to MAX_CYCLES every time, consuming tokens without improvement after the first cycle.

rule.statement: Use a structured critique rubric with explicit criteria and per-criterion scores; "could be better" feedback does not produce actionable revisions.
rule.rationale: Vague critique causes the revision pass to produce cosmetic changes only. Explicit criteria (accuracy, completeness, tone, length) yield targeted improvements.

section.reference: Structured critic prompt returning JSON pass/fail with defect list

section.name: tree-of-thoughts
section.title: Tree-of-Thoughts

rule.statement: Define a cheap heuristic evaluator first; using another LLM call as branch evaluator doubles cost per branch.
rule.rationale: ToT without pruning causes state-space explosion at depth 3+. An LLM evaluator that costs as much as generation makes ToT 2-4x more expensive than standard generation with no quality gain for most tasks.

section.p: Use ToT when: creative ideation, puzzle-like problems, strategic planning, or when a single reasoning path reliably fails. Cap tree depth at 3 for practical use.

section.name: multi-agent-topologies
section.title: Multi-Agent Topologies
section.p: Sequential: linear chain; low complexity. Parallel: concurrent execution; medium complexity. Supervisor/Coordinator: routing agent dispatches to specialists; medium complexity. Hierarchical: multi-level decomposition; high complexity. Swarm: all-to-all; highest complexity.

rule.statement: Each worker in a Supervisor pattern must have a well-defined scope and return structured output; open-ended worker responses cause the supervisor to misroute or re-assign the same task.
rule.rationale: Supervisor patterns fail silently when worker output is ambiguous. The supervisor interprets unstructured output as incomplete work and re-dispatches indefinitely.

rule.statement: For Supervisor patterns managing external actions (API calls, emails, database writes), add a human approval step before the supervisor dispatches to workers.
rule.rationale: Prevents cascading errors from misrouted tasks. One wrong dispatch can trigger irreversible side effects across multiple workers.

section.name: pattern-selection
section.title: Pattern Selection

rule.statement: Choose the simplest pattern that solves the problem: single ReAct agent before Plan-Execute, single-agent before multi-agent.
rule.rationale: Each pattern layer adds latency, cost, and failure modes. Complexity should be justified by a clear limitation of the simpler approach.

section.p: Decision path: simple single-step → direct prompt. External data/actions → Tool Use. Dynamic adaptive workflow → ReAct. Multi-phase with dependencies → Plan-Execute. Quality-critical → add Reflection. Multiple expertise areas → Multi-Agent.

section.name: implementation
section.p: Per-pattern implementation checklists covering observability, cost management, safety, and testing requirements.

rule.statement: Log pattern type, iteration count, tool calls, and model used alongside every agent output; this metadata is the only way to debug cost and quality regressions.
rule.rationale: Without structured logs, diagnosing "why did the agent take 15 iterations?" requires replaying the full trace. Metadata makes issues immediately visible in dashboards.

list.item: Log all LLM calls with inputs/outputs
Track token usage per component and per iteration
Monitor latency at each step
Implement tracing/spans compatible with LangSmith or Arize Phoenix
Categorize errors: tool failure, model timeout, max-iterations reached
Set token budgets per agent call; terminate gracefully when budget is exceeded
Implement early termination when partial results are sufficient
Use Haiku for mechanical steps, Sonnet for reasoning, Opus for planning/critique only
Cache repeated queries within a session
Monitor cost trends per pattern type to detect runaway loops
Implement content filtering on agent outputs before returning to users
Add human-in-the-loop approval for irreversible actions
Set action rate limits per tool to prevent runaway loops from causing side effects
Validate all tool outputs before injecting into the next prompt
Log all security-relevant events: tool rejections, max-iteration exits, error states

rule.statement: Test each pattern component in isolation before wiring them together; bugs compound in full agent traces and become hard to attribute.
rule.rationale: A prompt parsing bug in the Thought extractor causes every subsequent action to fail. Isolated tests catch this before it appears as an unexplained agent loop.

list.item: Unit test: individual components (tool, parser, prompt renderer)
Integration test: full agent flow on representative tasks
Edge case test: empty input, max iterations, tool failure, ambiguous task
Benchmark: compare agent quality against direct prompting baseline
Load test: verify cost and latency at production throughput

section.name: integration-guidance
section.p: When to use agent patterns in Claude subagents and agentic workflows.

rule.statement: Map Claude subagents directly to pattern roles: for ReAct, a single Claude subagent runs the Thought-Action-Observation loop with tools injected as functions; for Plan-Execute, spawn an Opus subagent as planner and Haiku subagents as executors; for Reflection, add a critic subagent that receives the draft and a structured critique rubric.
rule.rationale: Claude's native agent API enforces the pattern structure automatically. Combining patterns by nesting (ReAct within Plan-Execute steps, then Reflection on final output) maximizes quality without adding framework complexity.

rule.statement: Combine patterns by nesting: use ReAct for each step in a Plan-Execute workflow, and wrap the final output in a Reflection pass. Pattern selection should happen at system design time, not dynamically — switching patterns mid-execution adds complexity without gain.
rule.rationale: Each pattern has different cost/latency/quality tradeoffs. Choosing dynamically requires heuristics that are hard to maintain. Fixed pattern selection at design time is simpler and faster to debug.

section.note: Recommended subagents: `planner` (decomposes goal into ordered task list; use Opus for complex domains), `executor` (handles a single atomic task given inputs and tools; use Haiku for mechanical steps), `critic` (evaluates output against explicit criteria list, returns structured pass/fail with specific defects), `supervisor` (routes task to specialist worker agents, aggregates results, resolves conflicts), `verifier` (binary check: does the output satisfy all success criteria?).

template.purpose: ReAct system prompt with Thought/Action/Observation format
Structured critic prompt returning JSON pass/fail with defect list
Plan-Execute with model tiering (Opus planner, Haiku executor)
Tool definition schema for OpenAI/Anthropic function calling
